Adversarially review a generated LLD session. Be a skeptic — find REAL defects, do not rubber-stamp. Review-only: DO NOT modify files.

Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/f92b9828-3a48-4edc-8af4-8bd704a2f7dd_lld/  (source files + _response.md)
Run tests yourself: `cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/f92b9828-3a48-4edc-8af4-8bd704a2f7dd_lld && /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q`.

Problem: Design an Elevator System. Required distinct concurrency angle: SCAN movement algorithm + STATE machine per car (IDLE/MOVING/DOORS_OPEN/MAINTENANCE) + PLUGGABLE DISPATCH STRATEGY (nearest-car, least-busy) + PER-CAR lock so concurrent button presses on the same car serialise while different cars move in parallel; idempotent stop-queue (a set). Patterns: State, Strategy (DispatchStrategy), Template Method (_lock hook per car). Concurrency test: Barrier-sync many threads pressing the SAME floor on the same car plus distinct floors; assert the floor is queued exactly once (idempotent), all distinct stops are honored, and SCAN order is preserved — no lost or duplicated stop.

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence (file:line) on each:
1. CONCURRENCY CORRECTNESS: is the claimed angle actually implemented and correct? Can the invariant be violated (lost update, lost/duplicated message, double-dispense, negative stock, miscounted TTL)? Any deadlock, lost-wakeup (Condition not re-checked in a while loop / notify missed), or lock/permit leak? Does release/notify happen even when a callback or factory raises a NON-domain exception? Is slow I/O kept outside the lock?
2. CONCURRENCY TEST STRENGTH: does the test PROVE the invariant under real contention (Barrier + strict post-state: counts AND uniqueness AND typed losers AND committed-state)? Or could it pass trivially (work serialises, peak never reaches the limit, no hold in the critical section)? For scheduler/TTL: is the injected Clock used instead of real sleeps?
3. §3.5 API section present with a real typed contract table (Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose)? Every method also in §4.1/§4.2/§6?
4. §4.1: does EVERY class subsection end with a COMPLETE fenced class block after its (c) table (a method-less record still needs its fields block)?
5. Does the §6 ```python code match the actual disk files (no drift, no '...' bodies)?
6. EARN YOUR PATTERNS: are all claimed patterns genuinely realized in code (real State objects/transitions, real Strategy interface+impls, real Observer registration, real read-write lock)?
7. MERMAID: exactly 2 blocks; NO semicolon ';' inside any sequenceDiagram message text; both would parse in a real renderer.
8. Python: 3.11+ idioms only, no bare except, one domain exception base, no dead code.

Return verdict SHIP or FIX-FIRST and a findings list (severity/where/problem/fix). Only BLOCKER/MAJOR block; list MINORs too.

1	# Design an Elevator System
2	
3	> **Problem statement (verbatim):** Design the control logic for a bank of elevators: passengers make hall calls (UP/DOWN on a floor) and car calls (a destination inside a car); the system dispatches a suitable car and each car moves efficiently using the SCAN (elevator) algorithm without starving floors. Model each car as a state machine; dispatch policy is pluggable. Reject invalid floors/directions/unknown cars/out-of-service cars. Thread-safe under concurrent button presses; pressing the same floor twice is one stop (idempotent).
4	
5	---
6	
7	## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan
8	
9	| Time | What you do | Mode |
10	|------|-------------|------|
11	| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate the bank-of-elevators problem, ask clarifying questions (hall vs car calls, SCAN, pluggable dispatch). Don't write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
12	| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — `request_elevator`, `select_floor`, `tick`, `set_maintenance`, `pending_stops`. "Before classes, here's the contract external code calls." | 🗣️ SAY |
13	| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — 4 boxes: `ElevatorControlSystem`, `ElevatorCar` (state machine), `DispatchStrategy` + 2 concretes, `StopEvent`. Mention enums verbally. | ✍️ DRAW |
14	| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — State (car), Strategy (dispatch), Template Method (`_lock`). Then: "I see a check-then-orient race in `request_stop` — I'll fix it with a per-car lock in §9." | 🗣️ SAY |
15	| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — the key insight: car = self-guarding state machine + SCAN + stop-set (idempotent). | 🗣️ SAY |
16	| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (§6): `Direction`/`CarState` enums → `ElevatorCar` state machine + SCAN → `DispatchStrategy` + nearest → `ElevatorControlSystem` skeleton → `request_elevator()` → `tick()`. | ✍️ WRITE |
17	| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 2** — dispatch + SCAN order, then idempotent double-press. Run `main.py` (Uber coding env). | 🗣️ SAY / run |
18	| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-car lock via the no-op `_lock` hook; `ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem` overrides one method. Write subclass only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
19	| 55–60 min | **Follow-up questions** — hardest follow-ups from §9 (distributed dispatch, 10M buildings, sweep). | ❓ IF ASKED |
20	
21	**Two interview modes — pick one at the top:**
22	- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output. Demo cases verbally with state-transition tables. Write code, don't execute.
23	- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN `main.py` in §7. Show actual terminal output. This write-up is Mode B — the run is proven below.
24	
25	**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** Right after the diagram, proactively say "I see a race condition in how a car queues a stop and picks its direction — two presses on the same car can both orient from a half-updated queue. I'll keep the core clean and show the per-car-lock fix at the end." Say it before you're asked.
26	
27	---
28	
29	## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions
30	
31	### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)
32	
33	> 💬 "We're building the brain that runs a bank of elevators in a tall building. People standing on a floor press UP or DOWN — that's a 'hall call'. People already inside a car press a destination floor — that's a 'car call'. The system has to pick which car answers each hall call, and each car has to move smartly so nobody waits forever. The classic way a car moves is the SCAN algorithm: keep going one direction serving every stop on the way, and only turn around when there's nothing left ahead. I'm building the control logic, not the motors or the doors hardware."
34	
35	### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)
36	
37	**You:** "I'll model this as a *simulation* driven by a `tick()` call — each tick every car makes one SCAN move — rather than real wall-clock motion. That keeps it deterministic and testable. Sound right?"
38	**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's exactly what we want for an interview."
39	*✓ scoped: time advances via `tick()`, no real timers — fully deterministic.*
40	
41	**You:** "Core operations are: a hall call (`request_elevator(floor, direction)`), a car call (`select_floor(car_id, floor)`), advancing time (`tick`), and an admin maintenance toggle. Is that the full v1 surface?"
42	**Interviewer:** "That's the set. Maintenance is a nice touch — keep it."
43	*✓ four public operations; maintenance is in scope as a first-class mode.*
44	
45	**You:** "For the movement algorithm I'll use SCAN — the elevator goes one way until there are no more requests that way, then reverses. That's how real elevators and disk-head schedulers avoid starving the far end. Good?"
46	**Interviewer:** "SCAN is what I'm looking for. Make sure no floor starves."
47	*✓ SCAN named explicitly; anti-starvation is a hard requirement.*
48	
49	**You:** "Dispatch policy — which car answers a hall call — I'll make *pluggable* behind a strategy. Default is nearest-car; I'll also show a least-busy one. Reasonable?"
50	**Interviewer:** "Yes, pluggable dispatch is a big part of why this problem is interesting."
51	*✓ Strategy seam for dispatch; nearest-car default, least-busy alternative.*
52	
53	**You:** "Pressing the same floor twice should be one stop — idempotent. I'll back the stop queue with a set so duplicates collapse automatically. And under concurrent presses I'll make it thread-safe with a per-car lock. OK?"
54	**Interviewer:** "Perfect — that idempotency + thread-safety combo is exactly the edge I'd probe."
55	*✓ stop queue is a set (idempotent); thread-safety via per-car lock.*
56	
57	**You:** "I'll reject bad inputs with typed errors: a floor out of range, a nonsensical direction like UP-from-top, an unknown car id, and a car call to a car that's out of service. Each is its own exception. Agreed?"
58	**Interviewer:** "Agreed. Clear typed rejections are what I want to see."
59	*✓ five typed failure modes, all under one domain base.*
60	
61	### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)
62	
63	**Functional Requirements**
64	
65	1. **Passenger makes a hall call** via `request_elevator(floor, direction)`
66	   - System validates the floor is in range and the direction is sensible (no UP from top, no DOWN from bottom, not IDLE)
67	   - Dispatch strategy picks one in-service car; the floor is queued on it; returns the chosen car id
68	   - If no in-service car exists, reject with `NoCarAvailableError`
69	2. **Passenger makes a car call** via `select_floor(car_id, floor)`
70	   - Forces the destination onto THAT car (no re-dispatch — they already boarded)
71	   - Rejects unknown car id, out-of-range floor, and out-of-service car
72	3. **Each car moves by SCAN** — keep going one direction serving stops in order, turn around only when nothing remains ahead; every requested floor is served exactly once and no floor starves
73	4. **Pressing the same floor twice is one stop** (idempotent) — the stop queue is a set
74	5. **Time advances via `tick()`** — every car makes one SCAN move per tick and returns the stops that happened
75	6. **Admin can take a car in/out of service** via `set_maintenance(car_id, on)` — out-of-service cars are skipped by dispatch and reject car calls
76	7. **Thread-safe under concurrent button presses** — concurrent presses on the SAME car serialise; presses on DIFFERENT cars run in parallel; no lost or duplicated stop
77	
78	**Out of scope**
79	- Door/motor hardware control (we model state, not physics — out of system boundary)
80	- Real wall-clock timing / acceleration curves (we tick discretely — downstream concern)
81	- Passenger weight limits / overload sensors (a separate safety subsystem)
82	- Emergency / fire-recall modes (security feature, scoped out for v1 — would be a new `CarState`)
83	
84	> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK, before I model anything I want to lock down that this is a tick-driven simulation with SCAN movement and pluggable dispatch — that's the whole shape. With that, let me walk through the actors and flows."
85	
86	---
87	
88	## 2. Actors & Core Flows
89	
90	**Actors**
91	- **Hall passenger** — standing on a floor, presses UP or DOWN; triggers `request_elevator`.
92	- **Car passenger** — inside a car, presses a destination; triggers `select_floor`.
93	- **Simulation clock / driver** — calls `tick()` to advance every car one SCAN step (in production, a real timer thread).
94	- **Building admin** — takes a car in/out of service via `set_maintenance`.
95	
96	**Core flows**
97	1. Hall passenger: press UP on floor 3 → strategy picks nearest car → floor queued on that car → car id returned
98	2. Car passenger: inside car-A, press 8 → floor 8 queued on car-A (forced, no dispatch)
99	3. Driver: `tick()` → every car makes one SCAN move → stops that opened doors this tick returned as `StopEvent`s
100	4. Admin: `set_maintenance(car-A, on=True)` → car-A drops its queue, goes MAINTENANCE, vanishes from dispatch
101	5. Idempotency: press floor 7 three times on car-A → queued exactly once → served exactly once
102	
103	---
104	
105	## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
106	
107	### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)
108	
109	> **ElevatorControlSystem (the bank)** — The thing external code talks to: it owns all the cars, validates calls, and runs dispatch. Owns system-wide state (the car registry, the dispatch policy) and coordinates everything. *Accepted — this is the orchestrator.*
110	>
111	> **ElevatorCar** — A real thing with a current floor, a state (parked / moving / doors open / out of service), a direction, and its own set of pending stops. It has both data AND behavior — it runs its own SCAN move and guards its own state transitions. *Accepted — the state-machine entity, the heart of the design.*
112	>
113	> **DispatchStrategy** — The rule for "which car answers this hall call". It's pure policy with no state of its own, and the business will want to swap it (nearest today, least-busy tomorrow). That's a behavior worth its own abstraction. *Accepted — a Strategy interface with concrete implementations.*
114	>
115	> **StopEvent** — When a car opens its doors at a floor, we emit a small immutable record (which car, which floor, which direction). It has its own meaning as "a stop that happened" and lets callers verify SCAN order without poking car internals. *Accepted — a frozen value object.*
116	>
117	> **HallCall / CarCall** — Tempting to model a call as its own object. But a hall call is fully captured by `(floor, direction)` and a car call by `(car_id, floor)` — once dispatched, the only thing that survives is a floor in a car's stop set. A standalone Call class would just be a bag we throw away after queueing. *Rejected — modeled as method arguments; the surviving state is a floor in the car's set.*
118	>
119	> **Floor** — A real physical thing, but in the control logic it's just an integer in `[min_floor, max_floor]`. No behavior, no state of its own. *Rejected — modeled as an `int` with range validation.*
120	>
121	> **Direction** — A finite set: UP, DOWN, IDLE. No behavior. *Rejected as entity — modeled as an enum (shared vocabulary).*
122	>
123	> **CarState** — The four modes a car can be in. Finite, no behavior. *Rejected as entity — modeled as an enum that the `ElevatorCar` state machine guards transitions over.*
124	>
125	> **Passenger / Rider** — An actor, not stored state. They CALL the system; the system never holds a Passenger object. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*
126	>
127	> **Building** — The container. Its only relevant attributes (`min_floor`, `max_floor`) are config passed into the system. *Rejected — modeled as two ints on the orchestrator and each car.*
128	
129	### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)
130	
131	| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
132	|---|---|---|---|
133	| **ElevatorControlSystem** | Validate every public call and dispatch hall calls to a car | `_cars: dict[str, ElevatorCar]` (id → car), `_min_floor: int`, `_max_floor: int`, `_strategy: DispatchStrategy` (the pluggable policy) | Every public call reaches a defined outcome — queued on a valid in-service car, or rejected with a typed error |
134	| **ElevatorCar** | Own its physical state and pending stops; make one SCAN move per tick | `car_id: str` (**id**), `current_floor: int`, `state: CarState`, `direction: Direction`, `_stops: set[int]` (idempotent queue) | Only legal state transitions; a stop is served exactly once; same floor twice = one stop |
135	| **DispatchStrategy** | Pick ONE car for a hall call (or none) | *(interface — no state)* | Returns a car from the candidates given, or `None` — never invents a car |
136	| **StopEvent** | Immutable record of a door-open at a floor | `car_id: str`, `floor: int`, `direction: Direction` (frozen) | History never changes once recorded |
137	
138	**Enums** (shared vocabulary, not entities):
139	- `Direction` — `UP`, `DOWN`, `IDLE`
140	- `CarState` — `IDLE`, `MOVING`, `DOORS_OPEN`, `MAINTENANCE`
141	
142	### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro
143	
144	> 🎙️ "So the model has four objects. `ElevatorControlSystem` is the brain — it owns the cars by id and a dispatch strategy, and it's the only thing that validates inputs and routes hall calls. `ElevatorCar` is the interesting one: it's a state machine that owns its own pending stops as a set, and it runs its own SCAN move — the brain never reaches in and sets a car's state directly. `DispatchStrategy` is the seam — the brain asks it which car wins, so we can swap nearest-car for least-busy without touching dispatch. `StopEvent` is just a frozen receipt of a stop that happened. The pieces I deliberately left OUT are HallCall, CarCall, Floor, and Passenger — a call is just method arguments that collapse to a floor in a car's set, a floor is an int, and passengers are actors that call us, not state we store."
145	
146	---
147	
148	## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)
149	
150	| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
151	|---|---|---|---|---|
152	| `request_elevator` | `(floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str` | id of the assigned car | `InvalidFloorError`, `InvalidDirectionError`, `NoCarAvailableError` | A passenger on a floor presses UP/DOWN; the strategy picks a car and queues the floor |
153	| `select_floor` | `(car_id: str, floor: int) -> None` | — | `UnknownCarError`, `InvalidFloorError`, `CarOutOfServiceError` | A passenger inside a car picks a destination; forced onto that car |
154	| `tick` | `() -> list[StopEvent]` | the stops that opened doors this tick | — | Advance the simulation one step — every car makes one SCAN move |
155	| `set_maintenance` | `(car_id: str, on: bool) -> None` | — | `UnknownCarError` | Take a car in/out of service |
156	| `pending_stops` | `(car_id: str) -> frozenset[int]` | read-only snapshot of a car's queue | `UnknownCarError` | Inspect a car's stop queue (used to prove idempotency) |
157	
158	> 🎙️ "So the whole system is five public calls — two that take requests in (`request_elevator`, `select_floor`), one that drives time forward (`tick`), one admin toggle, and one read-only inspector. Everything else is internal: the cars move themselves by SCAN, the strategy picks the car. If I were handed this interface cold, I'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a single class."
159	
160	---
161	
162	## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)
163	
164	### 4.1 Per-class derivation
165	
166	#### 4.1.1 ElevatorControlSystem (the orchestrator)
167	
168	**(a) One-line role** — The system's public API: external code calls it to make hall/car calls, advance time, and toggle maintenance. Every operation flows through here.
169	
170	**(b) State derivation**
171	
172	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
173	|---|---|
174	| "dispatches a suitable car" / "unknown cars rejected" | A registry of cars keyed by id |
175	| "reject invalid floors" | The building's floor range |
176	| "dispatch policy is pluggable" | A reference to the current `DispatchStrategy` |
177	
178	```
179	class ElevatorControlSystem:
180	    - _cars: dict[str, ElevatorCar]
181	    - _min_floor: int
182	    - _max_floor: int
183	    - _strategy: DispatchStrategy
184	```
185	
186	*State placement trade-off:* A car's pending stops, current floor, and state are **physical** to the car — they describe the car's condition — so they live on `ElevatorCar`, not the orchestrator. The orchestrator only holds **relational/system** state: the registry and the policy. That keeps the orchestrator from duplicating per-car bookkeeping.
187	
188	**(c) Operations derivation**
189	
190	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
191	|---|---|
192	| "passengers make hall calls" | `request_elevator(floor, direction) -> str` |
193	| "car calls (a destination inside a car)" | `select_floor(car_id, floor) -> None` |
194	| "each car moves … SCAN" (advance time) | `tick() -> list[StopEvent]` |
195	| "out-of-service cars" | `set_maintenance(car_id, on) -> None` |
196	| "pressing the same floor twice is one stop" (inspect) | `pending_stops(car_id) -> frozenset[int]` |
197	| concurrency hook (Template Method) | `_lock(car) -> contextmanager` |
198	
199	```
200	class ElevatorControlSystem:
201	    - _cars: dict[str, ElevatorCar]
202	    - _min_floor: int
203	    - _max_floor: int
204	    - _strategy: DispatchStrategy
205	
206	    + ElevatorControlSystem(cars, min_floor, max_floor, strategy=None)
207	    + request_elevator(floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str
208	    + select_floor(car_id: str, floor: int) -> None
209	    + tick() -> list[StopEvent]
210	    + set_maintenance(car_id: str, on: bool) -> None
211	    + pending_stops(car_id: str) -> frozenset[int]
212	    # _lock(car) -> ContextManager   (no-op hook; thread-safe subclass overrides)
213	    # _require_car(car_id) -> ElevatorCar
214	    # _validate_floor(floor) -> None
215	    # _validate_hall_call(floor, direction) -> None
216	```
217	
218	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
219	- **Why does `request_elevator` return the car id?** So the passenger UI can light up "car A is coming". The actual stop lives inside the car; the id is the only thing the caller needs.
220	- **Why does `select_floor` force the car (no dispatch)?** Because the passenger already boarded — you can't re-route them to a different car. Dispatch only applies to hall calls.
221	- **Why a `_lock(car)` no-op hook?** The clean core has zero lock code. The thread-safe subclass overrides only this one method. The diff is ~10 lines.
222	
223	#### 4.1.2 ElevatorCar (the state machine)
224	
225	**(a) One-line role** — The only class allowed to change a car's state and the only owner of that car's pending stops; it runs its own SCAN move.
226	
227	**(b) State derivation**
228	
229	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
230	|---|---|
231	| "each car … current floor" | `current_floor: int` |
232	| "model each car as a state machine" | `state: CarState`, `direction: Direction` |
233	| "SCAN without starving floors" + "same floor twice is one stop" | `_stops: set[int]` (idempotent queue) |

237	class ElevatorCar:
238	    - car_id: str
239	    - min_floor: int
240	    - max_floor: int
241	    - current_floor: int
242	    - state: CarState
243	    - direction: Direction
244	    - _stops: set[int]
245	```

247	*State placement trade-off:* `_stops` is a **set**, not a list or a heap. SCAN doesn't need insertion order — it needs "is there a stop above/below me?", which a set answers, and the set gives idempotency for free. A list would let duplicates in; a heap would impose an order SCAN must override on every turnaround.

253	| queue a destination (idempotent) | `request_stop(floor) -> bool` (True if newly added) |
254	| advance one SCAN step | `step() -> StopEvent \| None` |
255	| take in/out of service | `set_maintenance(on) -> None` |
256	| "dispatch a suitable car" needs to know availability/load | `in_service() -> bool`, `is_busy() -> bool`, `pending_stops() -> frozenset[int]` |

262	    - max_floor: int

269	    + request_stop(floor: int) -> bool

271	    + set_maintenance(on: bool) -> None
272	    + in_service() -> bool
273	    + is_busy() -> bool
274	    + pending_stops() -> frozenset[int]

276	    # _reorient_after_stop(), _stops_in_current_direction(), _flip_direction(), _park()

279	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
280	- **Why does `request_stop` return a bool?** It returns True only if the floor was *newly* added. That boolean IS the idempotency signal — a duplicate press returns False and changes nothing.
281	- **Why does `set_maintenance(True)` clear the stops?** A car physically can't serve queued floors while out of service, so we drop them rather than strand them. Coming back resets to IDLE so the next call re-orients cleanly.
282	- **Why does only `step()` move `current_floor`?** Single source of truth for motion. The orchestrator never moves a car; it only ticks it.

284	#### 4.1.3 DispatchStrategy (the Strategy interface)
285	
286	**(a) One-line role** — The single seam behind which the "which car answers this hall call" policy lives.
287	
288	**(b) State derivation** — A strategy is pure policy; it holds no state. (No state block beyond the interface.)
289	
290	```
291	interface DispatchStrategy:
292	    (no fields — stateless policy)
293	```
294	
295	**(c) Operations derivation**
296	
297	| Need from requirements | Method on this interface |
298	|---|---|
299	| "dispatches a suitable car" / "dispatch policy is pluggable" | `select(cars, floor, direction) -> ElevatorCar \| None` |

303	    + select(cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None
304	
305	class NearestCarStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
306	    + select(cars, floor, direction) -> ElevatorCar | None   # closest car wins

312	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
313	- **Why does `select` get a pre-filtered list?** The orchestrator filters to in-service cars first, so a strategy only decides WHICH available car wins — it never has to know about maintenance.
314	- **Why can it return `None`?** If the candidate list is empty (all cars out of service), the strategy says "none" and the orchestrator turns that into `NoCarAvailableError`. No silent drop.

433	## 5. Design Patterns & Principles
434	
435	### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)
436	
437	> "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to the data it has. `ElevatorControlSystem` owns dispatch and validation because it's the only thing that sees ALL cars. `ElevatorCar` owns its own state transitions and SCAN movement because its floor, direction, and stop set are intrinsic to it — physical state belongs on the entity, not the orchestrator. `DispatchStrategy` owns the 'which car' decision because that's a pure policy with no state, and isolating it is what lets us swap it freely. The orchestrator never duplicates a car's movement logic, and a car never reaches out to peek at other cars."
438	
439	### 5.2 Applied patterns
440	
441	**State (the car's lifecycle)**
442	- **Why it fits HERE:** A car is in exactly one of IDLE / MOVING / DOORS_OPEN / MAINTENANCE, and only some transitions are legal (e.g. MAINTENANCE drops everything; a stop is only burned in DOORS_OPEN). Instead of `if car.state == "moving"` scattered across the orchestrator, the car guards its own transitions in `step()` and `set_maintenance()`.
443	- **Seam:** `ElevatorCar.state: CarState` + the transition methods (`request_stop`, `step`, `set_maintenance`) are the only writers of `state`.
444	- 🎙️ Script: "Each car is a state machine. The orchestrator never sets a car's state directly — it asks the car to move, and the car decides whether it parks, advances, or opens its doors. Illegal jumps are impossible by construction."
445	- 💬 Layman gloss: "It's like the car has its own little brain for whether it's parked, driving, or letting people in — the dispatcher just says 'take a step', it never reaches in and flips a switch."
446	
447	**Strategy (pluggable dispatch)**
448	- **Why it fits HERE:** "Which car answers this hall call" is exactly the thing a building will want to tweak — nearest-car for low traffic, least-busy during rush hour, maybe zone-based later. So I extract `DispatchStrategy` and the orchestrator never hardcodes the policy.
449	- **Seam:** `DispatchStrategy.select(...) -> ElevatorCar | None`, with `NearestCarStrategy` and `LeastBusyStrategy`.
450	- 🎙️ Script: "Dispatch is behind a strategy interface. The orchestrator filters to in-service cars and asks the strategy to pick one. Swapping policy is a one-line constructor change."
451	- 💬 Layman gloss: "The dispatcher doesn't decide which elevator to send — it asks a rule-of-the-day. We can change the rule without rewiring the dispatcher."
452	
453	**Template Method (the concurrency hook)**
454	- **Why it fits HERE:** The clean core must stay lock-free and readable. So the orchestrator calls a `_lock(car)` context manager that does nothing; the thread-safe subclass overrides only that hook with a real per-car lock.
455	- **Seam:** `ElevatorControlSystem._lock` (no-op) → `ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem._lock` (per-car RLock via the double-lock idiom).
456	- 🎙️ Script: "There's a placeholder method for locking. In the clean version it does nothing — zero lock code in that file. The thread-safe version is a subclass that replaces just that one method."
457	- 💬 Layman gloss: "There's an empty hook for locking. The safe version fills it in; the clean version leaves it empty. About ten lines change."
458	
459	### 5.3 SOLID principles
460	
461	- **Single Responsibility:** `ElevatorCar` owns physical state + SCAN, `DispatchStrategy` owns car selection, `ElevatorControlSystem` owns validation + coordination. Three reasons to change, three classes.
462	- **Open/Closed:** a new dispatch rule is a new `DispatchStrategy` subclass — zero core edits. A new car mode (e.g. fire-recall) is a new `CarState` value plus one guard.
463	- **Dependency Inversion:** `ElevatorControlSystem` depends on the `DispatchStrategy` abstraction, never on `NearestCarStrategy` directly — the concrete is injected.
464	- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem` is a drop-in for `ElevatorControlSystem` — same contract, only the `_lock` hook differs.
465	
466	### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS
467	
468	Every pattern above is in the §6 code: `CarState` + the car's transition methods (State), `DispatchStrategy` ABC with two concretes the orchestrator calls (Strategy), and the `_lock` no-op overridden by the subclass (Template Method). I did **not** claim Observer (no notification subscribers here — `StopEvent`s are returned synchronously, not pushed) or Factory (cars are constructed directly, no enum→object translation). Naming those would not survive `Cmd-F`.

471	
472	## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach
473	
474	### A. The Key Insight
475	
476	> **Insight:** Name the movement algorithm — it's **SCAN**. The car goes one direction serving every stop on the way until nothing remains ahead, then turns around. Back the stop queue with a **set** so it's idempotent, and make each car a **self-guarding state machine** so the orchestrator never touches a car's internals.
477	>
478	> **Why it works:** SCAN is correct because the building is a 1-D line of floors and a car moving one direction can serve every request on that side in one sweep — no request is skipped and the far end is always reached within one turnaround, so nothing starves. The "which floor next" question is answered purely from the set ("is there a stop above me?"), so order doesn't need to be stored. And pressing a floor is naturally idempotent because adding to a set that already has the element is a no-op.
479	>
480	> **What breaks without it:** The naive version uses a FIFO queue of stops and serves them in press-order. Now the car yo-yos — floor 2, floor 9, floor 3, floor 8 — wasting trips, and a floor pressed twice gets served twice. Worse, if the orchestrator inlines `car.state = MOVING` and writes the direction itself, six months later someone adds a maintenance mode and forgets to clear the queue in one path — now a car "out of service" still tries to serve floors. By making `ElevatorCar` a set-backed state machine that runs SCAN itself, both bugs are impossible by construction.
481	
482	### B. Per-Operation Algorithm
483	
484	**`request_elevator(floor, direction)` — the dispatch pipeline**
485	
486	*(1) Pseudocode:*
487	1. validate floor is in `[min_floor, max_floor]` → else `InvalidFloorError`
488	2. validate direction: not IDLE, not UP-from-top, not DOWN-from-bottom → else `InvalidDirectionError`
489	3. build candidate list = all cars where `in_service()`
490	4. `chosen = strategy.select(candidates, floor, direction)`
491	5. if `chosen is None` → `NoCarAvailableError`
492	6. under the per-car lock: `chosen.request_stop(floor)` (idempotent)
493	7. return `chosen.car_id`
494	
495	*(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script — the pipeline method:* "This is the pipeline — validate, dispatch, queue. Step 2 is where I reject nonsense directions; step 4 is where Strategy pays off — the orchestrator hands the strategy the in-service cars and gets one back, never knowing if it's nearest or least-busy. Step 6 is the critical section: it's wrapped in the per-car lock so two presses on the same car serialise, and `request_stop` is idempotent so a double-press is one stop. The edge the interviewer probes — 'what if all cars are out of service?' — is step 5: `select` returns None and we raise `NoCarAvailableError`, never silently dropping the call."
496	
497	*(3) DRY RUN — `request_elevator(floor=3, direction=UP)` with car-A at 0 (IDLE), car-B at 10 (IDLE), nearest-car strategy:*
498	
499	| Step | Action | State change |
500	|---|---|---|
501	| 1 | validate floor 3 in [0,10] | ✓ |
502	| 2 | direction UP, not top floor | ✓ |
503	| 3 | candidates = [car-A, car-B] (both in service) | — |
504	| 4 | `strategy.select(...)` → distance A=3, B=7 | returns car-A |
505	| 6 | under lock: `car-A.request_stop(3)` | car-A `_stops={3}`, state IDLE→MOVING, direction IDLE→UP |
506	| 7 | return `"car-A"` | caller gets `"car-A"` |
507	
508	> *Edge probe: what if a second passenger calls `request_elevator(3, UP)` while step 6 is mid-flight on car-A?* *Without a lock both could read `_stops={}` and both run `_orient_from_idle`, possibly committing different directions — that's the race I flagged. §9 fixes it with a per-car RLock around step 6.*
509	
510	**`select_floor(car_id, floor)` — the redemption/forced-queue path**
511	
512	*(1) Pseudocode:*
513	1. `car = _require_car(car_id)` → else `UnknownCarError`
514	2. validate floor in range → else `InvalidFloorError`
515	3. if not `car.in_service()` → `CarOutOfServiceError`
516	4. under the per-car lock: `car.request_stop(floor)`
517	
518	*(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script:* "`select_floor` is three guards and one queue. Unlike a hall call there's no dispatch — the passenger already boarded, so the floor is forced onto THAT car. Step 3 is the out-of-service guard the interviewer will probe; step 4 is the same idempotent, lock-guarded queue."
519	
520	**`tick()` — advance every car one SCAN step**
521	
522	*(1) Pseudocode:*
523	1. `events = []`
524	2. for each car: under its lock, `event = car.step()`; if event, append
525	3. return `events`
526	
527	*(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script — the driver method:* "`tick` is the clock. Each car steps under its OWN lock, so different cars move in parallel and the same car never double-steps. `car.step()` is where SCAN lives — it parks if empty, opens doors if it's standing on a requested floor, otherwise moves one floor toward the current direction and re-checks."
528	
529	**`ElevatorCar.step()` — one SCAN move (the algorithm core)**
530	
531	*(1) Pseudocode:*
532	1. if MAINTENANCE or no stops → `_park()`, return None
533	2. if `current_floor in _stops` → `_open_doors_here()` (burn it, emit StopEvent, re-orient), return event
534	3. if no stops in current direction → `_flip_direction()`
535	4. move one floor toward direction (clamped to the shaft ends)
536	5. if now `current_floor in _stops` → `_open_doors_here()`, return event
537	6. return None
538	
539	*(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script — the SCAN core:* "This is the heart. Step 1 parks an idle car. Step 2 handles the case where I'm already on a requested floor — burn it and open. Steps 3-4 are the SCAN rule: if there's nothing ahead the way I'm pointing, turn around, then take one step. Step 5 lets me open doors the same tick I arrive, so a stop is never skipped past."
540	
541	**`set_maintenance` / `pending_stops`** — `set_maintenance` flips the car's state (clearing stops on the way in); `pending_stops` is an O(1) read-only snapshot. Both trace to requirements 6 and 4 respectively.
542	
543	### C. Data Structure Choices
544	
545	| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
546	|---|---|---|---|
547	| `dict[str, ElevatorCar]` | car registry | car calls and admin toggles are by id; a list would be O(n) per lookup and an unknown id wouldn't be a clean miss | O(1) get |
548	| `set[int]` on each car | pending stops | gives idempotency for free (double-press collapses) and answers "stop above/below me?" directly; a FIFO list yo-yos and allows duplicates; a heap imposes an order SCAN must override on every turnaround | O(1) add/discard, O(n) "any above" scan, n = floors |
549	| `frozenset[int]` snapshot | `pending_stops` return | hand callers a read-only view so they can't mutate the car's private set | O(n) to build, n small |
550	| linear scan of candidate cars | dispatch | a bank is ~2-8 cars; a fancy index buys nothing at this scale | O(c), c = cars (tiny) |
551	
552	The "any stop above/below" scan is O(n) in floors, but n is the building height (tens), so it's a handful of comparisons per tick. *I'd switch the stop set to a sorted structure (two heaps, up-stops and down-stops) only if a single shaft served thousands of floors — never in a real building.*
553	
554	### D. Tricky Logic
555	
556	> **🧠 Claim:** Pressing the same floor twice must be exactly one stop (idempotent).
557	> **🔬 Proof:** With a FIFO list, two presses of floor 7 enqueue 7 twice; the car opens its doors at 7, serves it, and later opens AGAIN at 7 for the stale entry — a phantom stop.
558	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `_stops` is a `set`; `request_stop` does `self._stops.add(floor)` and returns `floor not in self._stops` (checked before the add). The second press returns False and changes nothing. The 40-thread test in §7 hammers this exact case.
559	
560	> **🧠 Claim:** A car must never skip past a requested floor.
561	> **🔬 Proof:** If `step()` moved a floor and returned without re-checking, a car heading up from 4 with a stop at 5 could move to 5 and then to 6 on the next tick without opening at 5.
562	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `step()` re-checks `current_floor in self._stops` immediately after `_advance_one_floor()` and opens there the same tick (pseudocode step 5).
563	
564	> **🧠 Claim:** The turnaround must be exact — turn only when nothing remains ahead.
565	> **🔬 Proof:** Turning too early strands the far end (starvation); turning too late runs the car into the shaft wall.
566	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `_stops_in_current_direction()` asks "any stop strictly above (UP) / below (DOWN) me?" and `_advance_one_floor` flips only when that's False, clamping at `min_floor`/`max_floor`.
567	
568	> **🧠 Claim:** Going into maintenance must not strand queued stops.
569	> **🔬 Proof:** If `set_maintenance(True)` left `_stops` populated, the car would resume those stops when it came back — serving requests passengers may have re-pressed on another car.
570	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `set_maintenance(True)` calls `self._stops.clear()` and sets direction IDLE; coming back sets state IDLE so the next call re-orients.
571	
572	> **🧠 Claim:** The compound `request_stop` (read set → add → orient if idle) must be atomic under contention.
573	> **🔬 Proof:** Two threads on an IDLE car both read `_stops={}`, both add, both run `_orient_from_idle`, and the second's direction/state write can clobber the first's based on a half-updated set — leaving the car pointed at nothing.
574	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** the orchestrator wraps `request_stop` in `with self._lock(car)`; the thread-safe subclass makes that a per-car RLock, so the whole compound op is one atomic step.
575	
576	### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example
577	
578	> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
579	> ```
580	> def select_floor(self, car_id, floor):
581	>     car = self._cars[car_id]          # bare lookup — KeyError leaks
582	>     car._stops.append(floor)          # list + direct field poke
583	> ```
584	> **Why it's wrong:** Three failure modes. (1) `self._cars[car_id]` raises a raw `KeyError` instead of a typed `UnknownCarError`. (2) `append` to a list lets duplicates in — pressing 5 twice is two stops. (3) Reaching into `_stops` directly bypasses the state machine, so the car never re-orients and an out-of-service car still accepts the stop. The correct version goes through `_require_car`, calls `car.request_stop(floor)` (set-backed, idempotent, self-orienting), and guards `in_service()`.
585

733	### `ElevatorCar` — the state machine + SCAN (`models.py`)
734	
735	Role: the only class allowed to change a car's state and the only owner of its pending stops.
736	
737	```python copy
738	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
739	
740	
741	@dataclass
742	class ElevatorCar:
743	    car_id: str
744	    min_floor: int
745	    max_floor: int
746	    current_floor: int = 0
747	    state: CarState = CarState.IDLE
748	    direction: Direction = Direction.IDLE
749	    # WHY: a SET, not a list. Pressing floor 5 twice must be ONE stop.
750	    _stops: set[int] = field(default_factory=set)
751	
752	    def in_service(self) -> bool:
753	        return self.state is not CarState.MAINTENANCE
754	
755	    def is_busy(self) -> bool:
756	        return bool(self._stops)
757	
758	    def pending_stops(self) -> frozenset[int]:
759	        return frozenset(self._stops)
760	
761	    def request_stop(self, floor: int) -> bool:
762	        # WHY: the ONE entry point that adds a stop. Returns True only if newly
763	        #      added — a duplicate press returns False (idempotency signal).
764	        if not self.min_floor <= floor <= self.max_floor:
765	            raise InvalidFloorError(
766	                f"floor {floor} outside [{self.min_floor}, {self.max_floor}] for car {self.car_id}"
767	            )
768	
769	        if self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE:
770	            raise CarOutOfServiceError(f"car {self.car_id} is out of service")
771	
772	        newly_added = floor not in self._stops
773	        self._stops.add(floor)
774	
775	        # WHY: a parked car that just got its first stop must pick a direction.
776	        if newly_added and self.state is CarState.IDLE:
777	            self._orient_from_idle()
778	
779	        return newly_added
780	
781	    def set_maintenance(self, on: bool) -> None:
782	        # WHY: in -> drop all stops (can't serve them); out -> back to IDLE.
783	        if on:
784	            self.state = CarState.MAINTENANCE
785	            self.direction = Direction.IDLE
786	            self._stops.clear()
787	        elif self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE:
788	            self.state = CarState.IDLE
789	            self.direction = Direction.IDLE
790	
791	    def step(self) -> StopEvent | None:
792	        # WHY: advance exactly one SCAN tick. Only place current_floor/state move.
793	        if self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE or not self._stops:
794	            self._park()
795	            return None
796	
797	        if self.current_floor in self._stops:
798	            return self._open_doors_here()
799	
800	        self._advance_one_floor()
801	
802	        # WHY: we may have landed on a requested floor — open this same tick so
803	        #      a stop is never skipped past.
804	        if self.current_floor in self._stops:
805	            return self._open_doors_here()
806	
807	        return None
808	
809	    def _orient_from_idle(self) -> None:
810	        target = min(self._stops, key=lambda f: abs(f - self.current_floor))
811	        self.direction = Direction.UP if target >= self.current_floor else Direction.DOWN
812	        self.state = CarState.MOVING
813	
814	    def _open_doors_here(self) -> StopEvent:
815	        # WHY: burn the stop so it can't be served twice, open doors, record event.
816	        self._stops.discard(self.current_floor)
817	        self.state = CarState.DOORS_OPEN
818	        event = StopEvent(self.car_id, self.current_floor, self.direction)
819	        self._reorient_after_stop()
820	        return event
821	
822	    def _advance_one_floor(self) -> None:
823	        # WHY: SCAN core — keep going while stops remain ahead, else flip.
824	        if not self._stops_in_current_direction():
825	            self._flip_direction()
826	
827	        self.state = CarState.MOVING
828	
829	        if self.direction is Direction.UP and self.current_floor < self.max_floor:
830	            self.current_floor += 1
831	        elif self.direction is Direction.DOWN and self.current_floor > self.min_floor:
832	            self.current_floor -= 1
833	
834	    def _reorient_after_stop(self) -> None:
835	        if not self._stops:
836	            self.direction = Direction.IDLE
837	            return
838	
839	        if not self._stops_in_current_direction():
840	            self._flip_direction()
841	
842	    def _stops_in_current_direction(self) -> bool:
843	        if self.direction is Direction.UP:
844	            return any(f > self.current_floor for f in self._stops)
845	
846	        if self.direction is Direction.DOWN:
847	            return any(f < self.current_floor for f in self._stops)
848	
849	        return False
850	
851	    def _flip_direction(self) -> None:
852	        if self.direction is Direction.UP:
853	            self.direction = Direction.DOWN
854	        elif self.direction is Direction.DOWN:
855	            self.direction = Direction.UP
856	        else:
857	            self._orient_from_idle()
858	
859	    def _park(self) -> None:
860	        self.state = CarState.IDLE
861	        self.direction = Direction.IDLE
862	```
863	
864	Reasoning: `request_stop` is the single mutation point — it validates, adds to the set, and orients an idle car. The `newly_added` boolean is the idempotency proof. Notice we re-check `current_floor in self._stops` AFTER moving in `step()` — that's what stops the car opening doors a tick late. The whole SCAN turnaround logic lives in three tiny helpers (`_stops_in_current_direction`, `_flip_direction`, `_reorient_after_stop`) so no single method gets deep.
865	
866	⚠️ "What if a car is on a requested floor when it goes into maintenance?" → "`set_maintenance(True)` clears `_stops`, so there's nothing left to serve. The next call re-orients it from scratch."

868	### `DispatchStrategy` + concretes (`strategies.py`)
869	
870	```python copy
871	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
872	
873	from models import Direction, ElevatorCar
874	
875	
876	class DispatchStrategy(ABC):
877	    @abstractmethod
878	    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
879	        """Pick ONE car for a hall call, or None if none is suitable."""
880	
881	
882	class NearestCarStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
883	    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
884	        if not cars:
885	            return None
886	
887	        # WHY: rank by (distance, busy?, id) — closest wins; idle beats busy; id breaks ties.
888	        return min(cars, key=lambda c: (abs(c.current_floor - floor), c.is_busy(), c.car_id))
889	
890	
891	class LeastBusyStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
892	    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
893	        if not cars:
894	            return None
895	
896	        # WHY: rank by (queue length, distance, id) — fewest stops wins.
897	        return min(cars, key=lambda c: (len(c.pending_stops()), abs(c.current_floor - floor), c.car_id))
898	```
899	
900	🎙️ "Two policies, one interface. Both rank with a tuple key and `min`, so ties break deterministically — same inputs always pick the same car, which makes tests trivial."

958	#### `request_elevator` — the dispatch pipeline
959	
960	Core logic: validate → build in-service candidates → strategy picks → queue under lock → return id.
961	Edge cases: bad floor, bad direction, all cars out of service.
962	
963	```python copy
964	    def request_elevator(self, floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str:
965	        self._validate_hall_call(floor, direction)
966	
967	        candidates = [car for car in self._cars.values() if car.in_service()]
968	        chosen = self._strategy.select(candidates, floor, direction)
969	
970	        if chosen is None:
971	            raise NoCarAvailableError(f"no in-service car for hall call at floor {floor}")
972	
973	        # WHY: hold the per-car lock only around the stop mutation, so two presses
974	        #      racing on the SAME car serialise and the floor is queued exactly once.
975	        with self._lock(chosen):
976	            chosen.request_stop(floor)
977	
978	        return chosen.car_id
979	```
980	
981	Reasoning: this is the pipeline — `_validate_hall_call` rejects nonsense before any state changes, the strategy fires on the in-service subset (the orchestrator never sees policy), and the only mutation (`request_stop`) sits inside the per-car lock. Notice we filter out-of-service cars BEFORE the strategy, so a strategy never has to think about maintenance. The `None` path is explicit — an unanswerable call raises, never returns silently.
982	
983	🎙️ "Validate, dispatch through the strategy, queue under the lock, return the car id."

1069	### Approach Comparison — `ElevatorCar.step()` (the most interesting method)
1070	
1071	**Approach 1 — FIFO queue, serve in press order (rejected):**
1072	```
1073	pop the oldest pressed floor, move straight to it, open doors
1074	```
1075	> **Challenges:** the car yo-yos — press 2, 9, 3, 8 makes it run 2→9→3→8, doubling travel and starving nobody but wasting everyone's time. And a floor pressed twice enqueues twice → a phantom second stop.
1076	
1077	**Approach 2 — recompute a full optimal route every tick (rejected for this scale):**
1078	```
1079	on every tick, solve "shortest tour covering all pending stops from current floor"
1080	```
1081	> **Challenges:** that's a traveling-salesman-flavored recompute every tick for zero benefit — on a 1-D line of floors SCAN is already optimal-ish, and the recompute is pure complexity with synchronisation risk. *I'd only revisit if cars could skip floors or had express zones.*
1082	
1083	**Chosen approach — SCAN over a set (one direction until empty, then flip):**
1084	```
1085	if on a stop -> open; elif stops ahead -> step toward them; else flip and step
1086	```
1087	> **Why it wins:** single source of truth (the set), O(1) idempotent presses, no starvation (the far end is reached within one turnaround), and the "next floor" question is a direct set scan — no stored order to keep in sync.

1089	### Verification — full-lifecycle dry runs
1090	
1091	**Scenario 1 — Happy path (dispatch + SCAN up):**
1092	Initial: car-A at 0, IDLE, `_stops={}`.
1093	
1094	| Step | Call | After |
1095	|---|---|---|
1096	| 1 | `request_elevator(3, UP)` → car-A | car-A `_stops={3}`, MOVING, UP |
1097	| 2 | `select_floor("car-A", 8)` | `_stops={3,8}` |
1098	| 3 | `select_floor("car-A", 5)` | `_stops={3,5,8}` |
1099	| 4 | tick ×3 | car-A reaches 3, opens → `StopEvent(car-A,3,UP)`, `_stops={5,8}` |
1100	| 5 | tick ×2 | reaches 5, opens → `StopEvent(car-A,5,UP)`, `_stops={8}` |
1101	| 6 | tick ×3 | reaches 8, opens → `StopEvent(car-A,8,UP)`, `_stops={}` |
1102	| 7 | tick | no stops → `_park()`, IDLE |
1103	
1104	Served order: `[3, 5, 8]` ascending. ✓
1105	
1106	**Scenario 2 — Idempotent double-press:**
1107	Initial: car-A at 0, `_stops={}`.
1108	
1109	| Step | Call | After |
1110	|---|---|---|
1111	| 1 | `select_floor("car-A", 7)` | `_stops={7}` (request_stop → True) |
1112	| 2 | `select_floor("car-A", 7)` | `_stops={7}` (request_stop → False, no change) |
1113	| 3 | `select_floor("car-A", 7)` | `_stops={7}` (False again) |
1114	| 4 | drain ticks | floor 7 emits exactly ONE `StopEvent` |
1115	
1116	One stop, served once. ✓
1117	
1118	**Scenario 3 — Race-prone scenario (plain English):** Without a lock, two passengers press buttons on an IDLE car-A at the same instant. Both threads enter `request_stop`, both see `state == IDLE`, both run `_orient_from_idle` reading a half-updated `_stops` — one could orient UP toward floor 9 while the other's write leaves `direction` DOWN toward floor 2, and the car ends up committed to a direction that matches neither stop. WITH the per-car lock, the second thread waits until the first finishes the whole add-and-orient, sees the car already MOVING, and just adds its floor to the set — consistent every time.
1119	
1120	### Complexity Summary
1121	
1122	| Operation | Time | Space | Key reason |
1123	|---|---|---|---|
1124	| `request_elevator` | O(c) | O(1) | scan c cars in dispatch (c = cars, tiny) + O(1) set add |
1125	| `select_floor` | O(1) | O(1) | dict lookup + O(1) set add |
1126	| `tick` | O(c · n) | O(c) | each of c cars does an O(n) "stop ahead?" scan (n = floors) |
1127	| `set_maintenance` | O(s) | O(1) | clears the car's stop set (s = pending stops) |
1128	| `pending_stops` | O(s) | O(s) | builds a frozenset snapshot |
1129

1132	## 7. Putting It Together (verified run + pytest suite)
1133	
1134	### 7.1 `main.py` — narrated demo output
1135	
1136	Running `python main.py`:
1137

1140	Case 1 dispatch + SCAN order [3, 5, 8] -> OK
1141	Case 2 idempotent double-press: floor 7 served exactly once -> OK
1142	Case 3 rejections (bad floor, bad direction, unknown car) -> OK
1143	Case 4 maintenance: dispatch skips out-of-service, rejects car calls -> OK
1144	Case 5 pluggable strategy spreads calls; SCAN turnaround [6, 2] -> OK
1145	All cases passed.

1147	
1148	Case 1 proves dispatch + ascending SCAN order. Case 2 proves idempotency (three presses, one stop). Case 3 proves the typed rejections. Case 4 proves maintenance removes a car from dispatch and rejects its car calls, and that an all-out bank raises `NoCarAvailableError`. Case 5 proves the pluggable least-busy strategy spreads calls and that SCAN turns around (start at 4, serve 6, then turn down to 2).
1149	
1150	### 7.2 `tests/test_elevator.py` — pytest suite
1151	
1152	Running `python -m pytest -q`:
1153	
1154	```text
1155	.....................                                                    [100%]
1156	21 passed in 0.01s
1157	```
1158	
1159	The suite has 21 tests: a happy-path per public method, every typed error path (`InvalidFloorError`, `InvalidDirectionError`, `UnknownCarError`, `CarOutOfServiceError`, `NoCarAvailableError`), SCAN turnaround and no-starvation, idempotency (both the orchestrator and the `request_stop` bool), maintenance behaviour, the pluggable strategy, and the two concurrency tests below.
1160	
1161	**The concurrency test (strict post-state):** 47 threads hit ONE car through a `threading.Barrier` so they all fire at the same instant — 40 press the SAME floor (must collapse to one stop) and 7 press distinct floors (all must survive). Assertions: no surprise errors; the queue equals EXACTLY the set of distinct floors pressed (no lost/duplicated stop); the car committed a CONSISTENT state (MOVING, direction UP toward a real pending stop — proving the compound check-then-orient was atomic under the lock); and SCAN then serves every floor exactly once in ascending order. A second test fires 20 threads across 4 different cars and asserts each car's queue holds exactly its own floors — proving different cars run in parallel without cross-contamination.

1164	
1165	## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)
1166	
1167	**Which flow and why:** I'm showing a hall call followed by ticks through to a door-open, because it touches every class — the orchestrator validates, the Strategy fires to pick a car, the car runs SCAN, and a `StopEvent` comes back.
1168	
1169	```mermaid
1170	sequenceDiagram
1171	    actor P as Passenger
1172	    participant Sys as ElevatorControlSystem
1173	    participant Strat as DispatchStrategy
1174	    participant Car as ElevatorCar
1175	    P->>Sys: request_elevator(3, UP)
1176	    Sys->>Sys: validate floor and direction
1177	    Sys->>Strat: select(in-service cars, 3, UP)
1178	    Strat-->>Sys: car-A (nearest)
1179	    Sys->>Car: request_stop(3) under per-car lock
1180	    Car->>Car: add 3 to stop set then orient UP
1181	    Car-->>Sys: True (newly added)
1182	    Sys-->>P: car-A
1183	    P->>Sys: tick()
1184	    Sys->>Car: step() under per-car lock
1185	    Car->>Car: move toward 3 then open doors at 3
1186	    Car-->>Sys: StopEvent car-A floor 3 UP
1187	    Sys-->>P: list with one StopEvent
1188	```
1189	
1190	> 🎙️ **Script — arrow by arrow:** "The passenger calls `request_elevator(3, UP)` on the system. The system validates the floor and direction itself, then delegates the 'which car' decision — it calls `select` on the strategy with only the in-service cars, and the strategy returns car-A. The interesting moment is `request_stop(3)` on the car, held inside the per-car lock — the car adds 3 to its set and orients itself UP; the orchestrator never touches the car's state. The car returns True (newly added), and the system hands the passenger car-A's id. Later a `tick()` calls `step()` on the car under the same lock — the car moves toward 3, opens its doors, and emits a `StopEvent`, which bubbles back as a one-element list."
1191	
1192	**Why the sequence shows the design is good:** the orchestrator coordinates but implements no business logic — the 'which car' decision delegates to the Strategy and the 'how to move' decision delegates to the car's SCAN state machine. That's Open/Closed and Information Expert at work in one flow.

1195	
1196	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1197	
1198	**DEFAULT: describe the solution; write code only if asked.**
1199	
1200	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "The race is in `request_stop`. It's three steps — read the stop set, add to it, and if the car was idle, pick a direction from the set. Two passengers press buttons on the SAME car at the same instant: both read the half-updated set, both run the orientation, and the second's direction/state write can clobber the first based on stale data. That's a check-then-act problem — the read and the writes aren't one atomic step."
1201	
1202	🗣️ **Step 2 — The solution:** "I add a lock PER CAR. Before queueing or stepping a car, the orchestrator takes that car's lock; while held, no other thread can mutate that car. The add-and-orient becomes one atomic step."
1203	
1204	🗣️ **Step 3 — The trade-off:** "Per-car locking, not one global lock, so ten different cars in the bank move fully in parallel — only presses on the SAME car serialise, and that's microseconds. The notify-style work (here, returning `StopEvent`s) happens outside the lock."
1205	
1206	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed case:** "Across multiple control servers an in-process lock isn't enough. I'd push atomicity to the shared store with a compare-and-set on the car's stop set — append the floor in a single conditional write — or route all calls for a given car to one owning server (consistent hashing on car id) so each car still has a single writer."
1207	
1208	**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY**
1209	
1210	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1211	|---|---|---|
1212	| **Race condition** | two threads touch shared state at once, result depends on timing | `request_stop`'s read-add-orient on one car |
1213	| **Critical section** | the block that must run as one atomic step | the add-and-orient inside `_lock(car)` |
1214	| **Check-then-act** | check a condition, then act, but state changed between | "saw IDLE, then oriented from a stale set" |
1215	| **Atomic** | all-or-nothing; no thread sees a half-done state | what the per-car lock guarantees |
1216	| **Mutex / lock** | a token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` per car |
1217	| **Reentrant (RLock)** | the SAME thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | a future guarded method calling another on the same car won't deadlock |
1218	| **Lock granularity** | how much one lock covers — coarse vs fine | we chose per-CAR, not one global |
1219	| **Pessimistic locking** | grab the lock before touching state | the in-process per-car RLock |
1220	| **Optimistic locking** | don't lock; write conditionally, retry on loss | the distributed compare-and-set |
1221	| **Compare-and-set (CAS)** | atomic "update only if value is still X" | the distributed conditional append |
1222	| **Deadlock** | two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided — we hold only ONE car lock at a time |
1223	| **Starvation** | a thread never gets the lock | not a risk — locks are held microseconds |
1224	
1225	**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A**
1226	
1227	> ❓ "Why per-car locks and not one global lock?"
1228	> "A global lock serialises the whole bank — car-A in the lobby would block car-B in the penthouse even though they share nothing. Per-car locking lets every car move in parallel; only presses on the SAME car serialise, and that's a microsecond set operation. I also keep the `StopEvent` collection outside the lock so nothing slow runs while it's held."
1229	
1230	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
1231	> "RLock is reentrant — the same thread can re-acquire it. Today no guarded method re-enters, but the moment a guarded method calls another guarded helper on the same car, a plain `Lock` would deadlock on the second acquire. RLock costs almost nothing and makes the design safe to extend."
1232	
1233	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1234	> "None here — a thread only ever holds ONE car lock at a time, and we never acquire a second lock while holding the first. No lock-ordering cycle means no deadlock. The map-lock in the thread-safe subclass is taken and released BEFORE the car lock, so it's not held across the critical section either."
1235	
1236	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1237	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny and same-car contention is low, so a short-held lock beats a retry loop. For the DISTRIBUTED case I flip to optimistic — a compare-and-set conditional write on the shared store — because you can't hold a Python lock across servers."
1238	
1239	> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-critical-section?"
1240	> "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so there's no orphaned lock — a supervisor restarts from the persisted car state. In the distributed CAS version, the conditional write either fully committed or didn't, so there's no half-done state to clean up."
1241	
1242	❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version**
1243	
1244	**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass:**
1245	```python copy
1246	import threading
1247	from collections.abc import Iterator
1248	from contextlib import contextmanager
1249	
1250	
1251	class ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem(ElevatorControlSystem):
1252	    def __init__(self, cars, min_floor, max_floor, strategy=None):
1253	        super().__init__(cars, min_floor, max_floor, strategy)
1254	        # WHY: a guard around the dict of locks so two threads creating a new
1255	        #      car's lock at once don't corrupt the dict.
1256	        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()
1257	        # WHY: RLock (reentrant) so the same thread can re-enter safely.
1258	        self._car_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
1259	
1260	    @contextmanager
1261	    def _lock(self, car) -> Iterator[None]:
1262	        # WHY: double-lock idiom — lock the map briefly to fetch-or-create the
1263	        #      car's lock, then hold ONLY the per-car lock for the critical section.
1264	        with self._map_lock:
1265	            car_lock = self._car_locks.setdefault(car.car_id, threading.RLock())
1266	
1267	        with car_lock:
1268	            yield
1269	```
1270	
1271	**Part B — the EXACT modified method.** The only change from §6 to §9 is which `_lock` runs — the method body is byte-for-byte identical because the lock is a hook:
1272	```python copy
1273	# ─── §6 version (clean, single-threaded) — _lock(chosen) is a no-op ─────────
1274	def request_elevator(self, floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str:
1275	    self._validate_hall_call(floor, direction)
1276	    candidates = [car for car in self._cars.values() if car.in_service()]
1277	    chosen = self._strategy.select(candidates, floor, direction)
1278	    if chosen is None:
1279	        raise NoCarAvailableError(f"no in-service car for hall call at floor {floor}")
1280	    with self._lock(chosen):          # ← no-op in §6
1281	        chosen.request_stop(floor)
1282	    return chosen.car_id
1283	
1284	# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — SAME body; _lock(chosen) now a real per-car RLock ─
1285	# (no method rewrite needed — the subclass overrides _lock, so request_elevator,
1286	#  select_floor, tick, and set_maintenance all become thread-safe unchanged)
1287	```
1288	
1289	🎙️ "The beauty of the hook is the diff is ZERO lines in the methods — `request_elevator`, `select_floor`, `tick`, and `set_maintenance` already call `self._lock(car)`. The subclass overrides only `_lock`, so all four become thread-safe with no edits. That's Template Method paying off."
1290	
1291	**Part C — prove it with the barrier test** (in `tests/test_elevator.py`, run green above):
1292	```python copy
1293	def test_concurrent_presses_same_floor_idempotent_and_no_lost_stops():
1294	    car = ElevatorCar(car_id="car-A", min_floor=0, max_floor=50, current_floor=0)
1295	    system = ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem([car], min_floor=0, max_floor=50)
1296	    duplicate_floor, duplicate_pressers = 7, 40
1297	    distinct_floors = [3, 11, 19, 25, 33, 41, 48]
1298	    total = duplicate_pressers + len(distinct_floors)
1299	    barrier = threading.Barrier(total)
1300	    errors = []
1301	
1302	    def press(floor):
1303	        barrier.wait()                       # all 47 hit at one instant
1304	        try:
1305	            system.select_floor("car-A", floor)
1306	        except BaseException as exc:
1307	            errors.append(exc)
1308	
1309	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=press, args=(duplicate_floor,)) for _ in range(duplicate_pressers)]
1310	    threads += [threading.Thread(target=press, args=(f,)) for f in distinct_floors]
1311	    for t in threads: t.start()
1312	    for t in threads: t.join()
1313	
1314	    expected = frozenset(distinct_floors) | {duplicate_floor}
1315	    pending = system.pending_stops("car-A")
1316	    assert errors == []
1317	    assert pending == expected            # floor 7 queued ONCE, all distinct survived
1318	    car_obj = system._require_car("car-A")
1319	    assert car_obj.state is CarState.MOVING and car_obj.direction is Direction.UP
1320	```
1321	
1322	🎙️ "The Barrier makes all 47 threads hit `select_floor` at the same instant — without it they'd take turns and there'd be no real race. The duplicate floor is queued exactly once, every distinct floor survives, and the car committed a consistent direction. That's the proof the per-car lock made the add-and-orient atomic."
1323	
1324	**Edge cases handled**
1325	- Floor out of range → `InvalidFloorError` → covered by `test_invalid_floor_rejected`
1326	- UP-from-top / DOWN-from-bottom / IDLE hall call → `InvalidDirectionError` → `test_invalid_direction_rejected`
1327	- Unknown car id → `UnknownCarError` → `test_unknown_car_rejected`
1328	- Car call to out-of-service car → `CarOutOfServiceError` → `test_car_call_to_out_of_service_rejected`
1329	- All cars out of service → `NoCarAvailableError` → `test_no_car_available_when_all_in_maintenance`
1330	- Same floor pressed twice → one stop (silent) → `test_pressing_same_floor_twice_is_one_stop`
1331	- Maintenance drops queued stops → `test_maintenance_drops_pending_stops`
1332	- SCAN turnaround / no starvation → `test_scan_turnaround...`, `test_scan_no_starvation...`
1333	- Concurrent same-car presses → consistent queue + state → `test_concurrent_presses_same_floor...`
1334	
1335	**Extensibility — interviewer twists**
1336	
1337	*(i) Level-calibration note:* the number of these tracks the target level — a junior gets none, mid-level one or two, senior several with depth. Here are three that prove the design bends without breaking.
1338	
1339	*(ii) Summary table:*
1340	
1341	| Want to add | How (one class/method) | What in the core changes |
1342	|---|---|---|
1343	| New dispatch policy (zone-based) | new `DispatchStrategy` subclass | nothing (inject it) |
1344	| Express car (skips some floors) | new `CarState`/skip-set on `ElevatorCar` | one guard in `step` |
1345	| Fire-recall / emergency mode | new `CarState.EMERGENCY` value | one branch in `step` |
1346	| Priority/VIP hall call | richer arg to `select` | one line in the strategy |
1347	
1348	*(iii) Twist walkthroughs:*
1349	
1350	> **Twist 1 — "Add a least-busy / zone-based dispatch policy."**
1351	> Currently dispatch is hardcoded to nearest-car only if you skipped the seam — but I built the Strategy seam exactly for this.
1352	> 🎙️ **What you say:** "Zero core change. I write a new `DispatchStrategy` subclass — say `ZoneStrategy` that maps floor ranges to preferred cars — and inject it in the constructor. The orchestrator still just calls `select`."
1353	> **The change:**
1354	> ```python
1355	> class ZoneStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
1356	>     def __init__(self, zones): self._zones = zones  # car_id -> (lo, hi)
1357	>     def select(self, cars, floor, direction):
1358	>         in_zone = [c for c in cars if self._zones[c.car_id][0] <= floor <= self._zones[c.car_id][1]]
1359	>         return NearestCarStrategy().select(in_zone or cars, floor, direction)
1360	> ```
1361	> **Trade-off:** zones improve rush-hour throughput but can leave a zone's cars idle while another zone queues — worth it in a tall mixed-use tower, overkill for a small building.
1362	
1363	> **Twist 2 — "Cars need a fire-recall / emergency mode that sends them to the lobby."**
1364	> Today a car has four states; emergency is a new one.
1365	> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I add an `EMERGENCY` value to `CarState`. Entering it clears the stop set, forces a single stop at the lobby, and blocks new calls — one new branch in `step` and a guard in `request_stop`."
1366	> **The change:**
1367	> ```python
1368	> # CarState gains EMERGENCY
1369	> def trigger_emergency(self, lobby: int) -> None:
1370	>     self.state = CarState.EMERGENCY
1371	>     self._stops = {lobby}
1372	>     self.direction = Direction.UP if lobby >= self.current_floor else Direction.DOWN
1373	> # request_stop adds: if self.state is CarState.EMERGENCY: raise CarOutOfServiceError(...)
1374	> ```
1375	> **Trade-off:** adds a state + one guard; mandatory for a real building's safety certification, scoped out of v1 here.
1376	
1377	> **Twist 3 — "Support an express car that only stops at floors divisible by 10."**
1378	> The car needs to ignore non-express floors.
1379	> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I give `ElevatorCar` a `stops_at(floor)` predicate (default: always True). `request_stop` rejects floors the car can't serve; the express car overrides the predicate. SCAN logic is untouched."
1380	> **The change:**
1381	> ```python
1382	> def request_stop(self, floor):
1383	>     if not self.stops_at(floor):           # express cars override this
1384	>         raise InvalidFloorError(f"car {self.car_id} does not serve floor {floor}")
1385	>     ...  # rest identical
1386	> ```
1387	> **Trade-off:** the dispatcher must route non-express requests to a non-express car — a strategy concern, not a core one.
1388	
1389	**Hardest follow-up questions**
1390	
1391	> ❓ "What's the hardest concurrency case in THIS design?"
1392	> "Two passengers pressing buttons on the SAME idle car at the same instant — the read-add-orient in `request_stop`. The per-car RLock makes that compound op atomic; the barrier test proves 40 same-floor + 7 distinct presses land a consistent queue and direction. Different cars never contend because each has its own lock."
1393	
1394	> ❓ "What if you have multiple control servers (distributed)?"
1395	> "An in-process lock only protects one process. I'd make each car's state authoritative in a shared store and either (a) route all calls for a car to one owning server via consistent hashing on car id — so each car has a single writer — or (b) use an optimistic compare-and-set when appending a stop. Both keep the per-car atomicity guarantee across servers."
1396	
1397	> ❓ "What if you have 10M buildings / a huge bank?"
1398	> "Dispatch is O(cars-in-this-bank), which is tiny — banks have a handful of cars. Scale is horizontal across buildings: each building is an independent shard with its own `ElevatorControlSystem`, partitioned by building id. Nothing in the core couples one building to another, so it scales linearly."
1399	
1400	> ❓ "What if a `tick` fails halfway — a car's motor faults mid-move?"
1401	> "Each car steps independently under its own lock, so one car faulting doesn't corrupt others. I'd catch the fault, put that car into MAINTENANCE (which drops its queue so passengers re-request on another car via dispatch), and continue ticking the rest. The compensation is built in — a faulted car simply leaves the dispatch pool."
1402	
1403	> 🎙️ **Final 30-second summary:** "The key insight is naming the algorithm — SCAN — and making each car a self-guarding state machine that owns its stops as a set, so a double-press is one stop by construction and no floor starves. The orchestrator coordinates through one Strategy seam — `DispatchStrategy`, with nearest-car and least-busy concretes — so new dispatch rules are new classes, the core never changes. The one concurrency risk is the read-add-orient in `request_stop`; I guard it with a per-car RLock via a Template-Method hook, proved by a 47-thread barrier test. For distributed scale, I'd push the per-car atomicity to a compare-and-set or route each car to a single owning server."
1404
