1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""
3	models.py — the "nouns" of the elevator system: enums, exceptions, and the
4	ElevatorCar state machine.
5	
6	The ONE smart move in this problem: each car is a self-guarding STATE MACHINE
7	(IDLE / MOVING / DOORS_OPEN / MAINTENANCE) that holds its own set of pending
8	stops, and the "stop queue" is a SET so pressing the same floor twice is one
9	stop (idempotent by construction). The car decides its own next move using the
10	SCAN algorithm — keep going one direction until no more requests that way, then
11	turn around — so no floor starves.
12	"""
13	
14	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
15	from enum import Enum
16	
17	
18	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
19	# Direction is shared vocabulary: a hall call is UP or DOWN, and a moving car is
20	# travelling UP or DOWN. IDLE means "no committed direction right now".
21	# 🎙️ "Direction is just the three values a car can be heading — up, down, or
22	#     parked. I keep it as one enum so the SCAN logic reads in plain words."
23	class Direction(Enum):
24	    UP = "UP"
25	    DOWN = "DOWN"
26	    IDLE = "IDLE"
27	
28	
29	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
30	# CarState is the heart of the design. A car can only be in one of these four
31	# states, and only a handful of transitions are legal. Putting them in an enum
32	# lets the car guard its own transitions instead of scattering string checks.
33	# 🎙️ "Each car is a little state machine. It's parked, moving, holding the doors
34	#     open, or pulled out for maintenance — nothing else. Illegal jumps are
35	#     impossible because the car owns the transition."
36	class CarState(Enum):
37	    IDLE = "IDLE"
38	    MOVING = "MOVING"
39	    DOORS_OPEN = "DOORS_OPEN"
40	    MAINTENANCE = "MAINTENANCE"
41	
42	
43	# ── WHY ONE BASE EXCEPTION ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
44	# One domain base means callers can catch "anything this system rejected" with a
45	# single except, while each subclass names exactly one failure mode so a test can
46	# assert the precise reason. We NEVER raise bare ValueError/RuntimeError.
47	class ElevatorError(Exception):
48	    """Base class for every error this elevator system raises."""
49	
50	
51	class InvalidFloorError(ElevatorError):
52	    """A floor outside the building's [min_floor, max_floor] range."""
53	
54	
55	class InvalidDirectionError(ElevatorError):
56	    """A hall call whose direction makes no sense (e.g. UP on the top floor, or IDLE)."""
57	
58	
59	class UnknownCarError(ElevatorError):
60	    """A car id the system has never heard of."""
61	
62	
63	class CarOutOfServiceError(ElevatorError):
64	    """A car call (destination) sent to a car that is in MAINTENANCE."""
65	
66	
67	class NoCarAvailableError(ElevatorError):
68	    """A hall call that no in-service car could be assigned to."""
69	
70	
71	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72	# StopEvent is the immutable record we emit every time a car actually stops and
73	# opens its doors. It is frozen because a stop that already happened must never
74	# be edited — it is history. The caller (or a test) reads these to verify SCAN
75	# order without reaching into car internals.
76	# 🎙️ "Every time a car opens its doors I hand back a small frozen record — which
77	#     car, which floor, which way it was heading. That's the observable proof the
78	#     SCAN order was honoured."
79	@dataclass(frozen=True)
80	class StopEvent:
81	    car_id: str
82	    floor: int
83	    direction: Direction
84	
85	
86	# ── WHY THIS CLASS (the state machine) ───────────────────────────────────────
87	# ElevatorCar is the single thing allowed to change a car's status and the single
88	# owner of that car's pending stops. The orchestrator NEVER writes car.state
89	# directly — it calls request_stop / step, and the car guards itself. This is why
90	# illegal state jumps and lost/duplicated stops are impossible by construction.
91	# 🎙️ "This is the most interesting class. It's a state machine that owns its own
92	#     stop set. The dispatcher pushes stops in and ticks it forward; the car
93	#     decides — using SCAN — which floor it visits next and when it turns around."
94	@dataclass
95	class ElevatorCar:
96	    car_id: str
97	    min_floor: int
98	    max_floor: int
99	    current_floor: int = 0
100	    state: CarState = CarState.IDLE
101	    direction: Direction = Direction.IDLE
102	    # WHY: a SET, not a list. Pressing floor 5 twice must be ONE stop. A set
103	    #      drops the duplicate automatically — idempotency by construction.
104	    _stops: set[int] = field(default_factory=set)
105	
106	    def in_service(self) -> bool:
107	        # WHY: a car in MAINTENANCE is invisible to dispatch and rejects car calls.
108	        return self.state is not CarState.MAINTENANCE
109	
110	    def is_busy(self) -> bool:
111	        # WHY: "busy" = has work to do. Used by the least-busy dispatch strategy.
112	        return bool(self._stops)
113	
114	    def pending_stops(self) -> frozenset[int]:
115	        # WHY: hand out a read-only snapshot so callers/tests can inspect the
116	        #      queue without being able to mutate the car's private set.
117	        return frozenset(self._stops)
118	
119	    def request_stop(self, floor: int) -> bool:
120	        # WHY: the ONE entry point that adds a stop. Returns True only if this
121	        #      floor was newly added — pressing the same floor again returns
122	        #      False (the set already had it). That boolean IS the idempotency
123	        #      signal the dispatcher relies on.
124	        if not self.min_floor <= floor <= self.max_floor:
125	            raise InvalidFloorError(
126	                f"floor {floor} outside [{self.min_floor}, {self.max_floor}] for car {self.car_id}"
127	            )
128	
129	        if self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE:
130	            raise CarOutOfServiceError(f"car {self.car_id} is out of service")
131	
132	        newly_added = floor not in self._stops
133	        self._stops.add(floor)
134	
135	        # WHY: a parked car that just got its first stop must pick a direction so
136	        #      SCAN has something to sweep toward. We only seed direction when
137	        #      IDLE — never override a sweep already in progress.
138	        if newly_added and self.state is CarState.IDLE:
139	            self._orient_from_idle()
140	
141	        return newly_added
142	
143	    def set_maintenance(self, on: bool) -> None:
144	        # WHY: maintenance is a first-class mode. Going IN drops all pending stops
145	        #      (the car physically can't serve them); coming OUT returns to IDLE so
146	        #      the next call re-orients it cleanly.
147	        if on:
148	            self.state = CarState.MAINTENANCE
149	            self.direction = Direction.IDLE
150	            self._stops.clear()
151	        elif self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE:
152	            self.state = CarState.IDLE
153	            self.direction = Direction.IDLE
154	
155	    def step(self) -> StopEvent | None:
156	        # WHY: advance this car exactly one tick of the SCAN algorithm. Returns a
157	        #      StopEvent if the car opened its doors this tick, else None. This is
158	        #      the only place current_floor / state move forward.
159	        if self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE or not self._stops:
160	            self._park()
161	            return None
162	
163	        if self.current_floor in self._stops:
164	            return self._open_doors_here()
165	
166	        self._advance_one_floor()
167	
168	        # WHY: after moving we may have landed on a requested floor — open there
169	        #      this same tick so a stop is never skipped past.
170	        if self.current_floor in self._stops:
171	            return self._open_doors_here()
172	
173	        return None
174	
175	    # ── private SCAN helpers (the elevator algorithm) ────────────────────────
176	
177	    def _orient_from_idle(self) -> None:
178	        # WHY: from a dead stop, head toward the nearest pending stop. Tie or
179	        #      target-above → UP, target-below → DOWN. Keeps the first move sane.
180	        target = min(self._stops, key=lambda f: abs(f - self.current_floor))
181	        self.direction = Direction.UP if target >= self.current_floor else Direction.DOWN
182	        self.state = CarState.MOVING
183	
184	    def _open_doors_here(self) -> StopEvent:
185	        # WHY: arriving at a requested floor is a real stop — burn it from the set
186	        #      (so it can't be served twice), open the doors, record the event.
187	        self._stops.discard(self.current_floor)
188	        self.state = CarState.DOORS_OPEN
189	        event = StopEvent(self.car_id, self.current_floor, self.direction)
190	        self._reorient_after_stop()
191	        return event
192	
193	    def _advance_one_floor(self) -> None:
194	        # WHY: this is SCAN's core rule — keep going the current way while stops
195	        #      remain that way; otherwise flip. Never run past the ends of the shaft.
196	        if not self._stops_in_current_direction():
197	            self._flip_direction()
198	
199	        self.state = CarState.MOVING
200	
201	        if self.direction is Direction.UP and self.current_floor < self.max_floor:
202	            self.current_floor += 1
203	        elif self.direction is Direction.DOWN and self.current_floor > self.min_floor:
204	            self.current_floor -= 1
205	
206	    def _reorient_after_stop(self) -> None:
207	        # WHY: after serving a floor, decide what's next. No stops → park. Stops
208	        #      still ahead the current way → keep that way. Otherwise turn around.
209	        if not self._stops:
210	            self.direction = Direction.IDLE
211	            return
212	
213	        if not self._stops_in_current_direction():
214	            self._flip_direction()
215	
216	    def _stops_in_current_direction(self) -> bool:
217	        # WHY: "is there anything still to serve the way I'm pointing?" — the
218	        #      single question SCAN asks before every move.
219	        if self.direction is Direction.UP:
220	            return any(f > self.current_floor for f in self._stops)
221	
222	        if self.direction is Direction.DOWN:
223	            return any(f < self.current_floor for f in self._stops)
224	
225	        return False
226	
227	    def _flip_direction(self) -> None:
228	        # WHY: the turnaround. UP↔DOWN; if somehow IDLE, re-orient from scratch.
229	        if self.direction is Direction.UP:
230	            self.direction = Direction.DOWN
231	        elif self.direction is Direction.DOWN:
232	            self.direction = Direction.UP
233	        else:
234	            self._orient_from_idle()
235	
236	    def _park(self) -> None:
237	        # WHY: no work left — settle to IDLE with no committed direction. Idempotent:
238	        #      calling it on an already-parked car just re-confirms the same state.
239	        self.state = CarState.IDLE
240	        self.direction = Direction.IDLE
241

2	"""
3	strategies.py — the pluggable DISPATCH policy.
4	
5	The dispatcher never hardcodes HOW to pick a car for a hall call. It asks a
6	DispatchStrategy. Today we want "nearest suitable car"; tomorrow the building
7	ops team wants "spread the load — least-busy car". Both plug in as one class
8	with zero changes to the orchestrator. That's the Strategy seam.
9	"""
10	
11	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
12	
13	from models import Direction, ElevatorCar
14	
15	
16	# ── WHY THIS INTERFACE ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
17	# DispatchStrategy is the single seam the building's policy lives behind. The
18	# orchestrator depends on this abstraction, never on a concrete rule — so we can
19	# swap nearest-car for least-busy without touching dispatch logic.
20	# 🎙️ "The dispatcher doesn't know if we're using nearest-car or least-busy. It
21	#     hands the strategy the candidate cars and the call, and gets back one car."
22	class DispatchStrategy(ABC):
23	    @abstractmethod
24	    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
25	        """Pick ONE car for a hall call, or None if none is suitable.
26	
27	        `cars` is already filtered to in-service cars by the orchestrator, so a
28	        strategy only worries about WHICH of the available cars wins.
29	        """
30	
31	
32	# ── WHY THIS CONCRETE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
33	# NearestCarStrategy is the default real-world policy: send the closest car so
34	# the passenger waits least. We add a tiny tie-breaker so idle cars beat busy
35	# cars at equal distance, and the car id breaks remaining ties deterministically.
36	class NearestCarStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
37	    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
38	        if not cars:
39	            return None
40	
41	        # WHY: rank by (distance, busy?, id). Closest wins; among equally close,
42	        #      an idle car wins over a busy one; id makes it deterministic so the
43	        #      same inputs always pick the same car (great for tests).
44	        return min(
45	            cars,
46	            key=lambda c: (abs(c.current_floor - floor), c.is_busy(), c.car_id),
47	        )
48	
49	
50	# ── WHY THIS CONCRETE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
51	# LeastBusyStrategy spreads load: pick the car with the fewest pending stops so no
52	# single car becomes a bottleneck during a morning rush. Distance is the
53	# tie-breaker so a lightly-loaded faraway car doesn't beat a free nearby one.
54	class LeastBusyStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
55	    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
56	        if not cars:
57	            return None
58	
59	        # WHY: rank by (queue length, distance, id). Fewest stops wins; ties go to
60	        #      the closer car, then id for determinism.
61	        return min(
62	            cars,
63	            key=lambda c: (len(c.pending_stops()), abs(c.current_floor - floor), c.car_id),
64	        )
65

2	"""
3	elevator_service.py — the orchestrator (the "brain") plus its thread-safe twin.
4	
5	ElevatorControlSystem owns the bank of cars and the dispatch policy. It validates
6	every public call, routes hall calls to a car via the strategy, forwards car calls
7	to the named car, and drives time forward one tick at a time.
8	
9	The clean core is single-threaded and lock-free: the `_lock(car)` hook is a no-op
10	context manager. ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem is the §9 second version — it
11	overrides ONLY that hook to give each car its own lock, so concurrent button
12	presses on the SAME car serialise while DIFFERENT cars move in parallel.
13	"""
14	
15	import threading
16	from collections.abc import Iterator
17	from contextlib import contextmanager
18	
19	from models import (
20	    CarOutOfServiceError,
21	    Direction,
22	    ElevatorCar,
23	    InvalidDirectionError,
24	    InvalidFloorError,
25	    NoCarAvailableError,
26	    StopEvent,
27	    UnknownCarError,
28	)
29	from strategies import DispatchStrategy, NearestCarStrategy
30	
31	
32	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
33	# ElevatorControlSystem is the system's public API — every passenger action and
34	# every admin action flows through here. It is the only thing that sees ALL cars,
35	# so it is the right place to validate inputs and to dispatch hall calls. It never
36	# changes a car's internal state directly; it asks the car to do it.
37	# 🎙️ "This is the brain. It holds the cars and a dispatch strategy. Hall calls go
38	#     through the strategy to pick a car; car calls go straight to the named car;
39	#     tick() steps every car one SCAN move."
40	class ElevatorControlSystem:
41	    def __init__(
42	        self,
43	        cars: list[ElevatorCar],
44	        min_floor: int,
45	        max_floor: int,
46	        strategy: DispatchStrategy | None = None,
47	    ) -> None:
48	        if min_floor >= max_floor:
49	            raise InvalidFloorError(f"min_floor {min_floor} must be below max_floor {max_floor}")
50	
51	        # WHY: index cars by id so car calls and admin toggles are O(1) lookups and
52	        #      an unknown id is an instant, clean rejection.
53	        self._cars: dict[str, ElevatorCar] = {car.car_id: car for car in cars}
54	        self._min_floor = min_floor
55	        self._max_floor = max_floor
56	        # WHY: default to nearest-car, but accept any strategy — that's the seam.
57	        self._strategy: DispatchStrategy = strategy or NearestCarStrategy()
58	
59	    # ── concurrency hook (Template Method) ───────────────────────────────────
60	    @contextmanager
61	    def _lock(self, car: ElevatorCar) -> Iterator[None]:
62	        # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version — there are zero locks in
63	        #      this file. The thread-safe subclass overrides just this one method
64	        #      to wrap a real per-car lock. The diff is about ten lines.
65	        yield
66	
67	    # ── public API ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
68	
69	    def request_elevator(self, floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str:
70	        # WHY: a passenger on a floor presses UP/DOWN. We validate, ask the
71	        #      strategy for a car, queue the floor on it, and return the car id.
72	        self._validate_hall_call(floor, direction)
73	
74	        candidates = [car for car in self._cars.values() if car.in_service()]
75	        chosen = self._strategy.select(candidates, floor, direction)
76	
77	        if chosen is None:
78	            raise NoCarAvailableError(f"no in-service car for hall call at floor {floor}")
79	
80	        # WHY: hold the per-car lock only around the stop mutation, so two presses
81	        #      racing on the SAME car serialise and the floor is queued exactly once.
82	        with self._lock(chosen):
83	            chosen.request_stop(floor)
84	
85	        return chosen.car_id
86	
87	    def select_floor(self, car_id: str, floor: int) -> None:
88	        # WHY: a passenger INSIDE a car picks a destination. The destination is
89	        #      forced onto THAT car (no dispatch) — you can't re-route someone who
90	        #      already boarded.
91	        car = self._require_car(car_id)
92	        self._validate_floor(floor)
93	
94	        if not car.in_service():
95	            raise CarOutOfServiceError(f"car {car_id} is out of service")
96	
97	        # WHY: same per-car lock — concurrent in-car presses on this car serialise.
98	        with self._lock(car):
99	            car.request_stop(floor)
100	
101	    def tick(self) -> list[StopEvent]:
102	        # WHY: advance the whole simulation one step. Every car takes one SCAN move
103	        #      under its OWN lock, so cars step independently — different cars move
104	        #      in parallel, the same car never double-steps.
105	        events: list[StopEvent] = []
106	
107	        for car in self._cars.values():
108	
109	            with self._lock(car):
110	                event = car.step()
111	
112	            if event is not None:
113	                events.append(event)
114	
115	        return events
116	
117	    def set_maintenance(self, car_id: str, on: bool) -> None:
118	        # WHY: take a car in or out of service. Out-of-service cars are skipped by
119	        #      dispatch and reject car calls until they come back.
120	        car = self._require_car(car_id)
121	
122	        with self._lock(car):
123	            car.set_maintenance(on)
124	
125	    def pending_stops(self, car_id: str) -> frozenset[int]:
126	        # WHY: read-only inspection of a car's queue — used by main.py and tests to
127	        #      prove idempotency (same floor twice = one stop) without poking internals.
128	        return self._require_car(car_id).pending_stops()
129	
130	    # ── private validation helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────
131	
132	    def _require_car(self, car_id: str) -> ElevatorCar:
133	        # WHY: one place that turns an unknown id into a typed error instead of a
134	        #      raw KeyError leaking out of a dict access.
135	        car = self._cars.get(car_id)
136	
137	        if car is None:
138	            raise UnknownCarError(f"no car with id {car_id!r}")
139	
140	        return car
141	
142	    def _validate_floor(self, floor: int) -> None:
143	        if not self._min_floor <= floor <= self._max_floor:
144	            raise InvalidFloorError(
145	                f"floor {floor} outside [{self._min_floor}, {self._max_floor}]"
146	            )
147	
148	    def _validate_hall_call(self, floor: int, direction: Direction) -> None:
149	        # WHY: group the hall-call guards so request_elevator stays short. A hall
150	        #      call must be a real floor and a real travel direction — you cannot
151	        #      press UP on the top floor or DOWN on the bottom, and IDLE is not a call.
152	        self._validate_floor(floor)
153	
154	        if direction is Direction.IDLE:
155	            raise InvalidDirectionError("hall call direction cannot be IDLE")
156	
157	        if direction is Direction.UP and floor == self._max_floor:
158	            raise InvalidDirectionError(f"cannot call UP from top floor {floor}")
159	
160	        if direction is Direction.DOWN and floor == self._min_floor:
161	            raise InvalidDirectionError(f"cannot call DOWN from bottom floor {floor}")
162	
163	
164	# ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
165	# ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem is the §9 hardened version. It changes NOTHING
166	# about the elevator logic — it overrides only the `_lock` hook so each car gets
167	# its OWN reentrant lock. Concurrent presses on the same car serialise; presses on
168	# different cars run fully in parallel. This is the per-car lock the problem asks for.
169	# 🎙️ "The thread-safe version is a subclass that overrides one method. Each car
170	#     has its own lock, so a busy lobby car doesn't block a quiet penthouse car."
171	class ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem(ElevatorControlSystem):
172	    def __init__(
173	        self,
174	        cars: list[ElevatorCar],
175	        min_floor: int,
176	        max_floor: int,
177	        strategy: DispatchStrategy | None = None,
178	    ) -> None:
179	        super().__init__(cars, min_floor, max_floor, strategy)
180	        # WHY: a guard around the dict of locks. Two threads pressing a brand-new
181	        #      car's button at once must not corrupt the dict while inserting its lock.
182	        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()
183	        # WHY: RLock (reentrant) so the SAME thread can re-enter — e.g. a future
184	        #      guarded method that calls another guarded method on the same car
185	        #      won't deadlock on the second acquire.
186	        self._car_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
187	
188	    @contextmanager
189	    def _lock(self, car: ElevatorCar) -> Iterator[None]:
190	        # WHY: the double-lock idiom. First lock the map to fetch-or-create this
191	        #      car's lock, then release the map and hold only the per-car lock for
192	        #      the critical section. Map lock is held for microseconds.
193	        with self._map_lock:
194	            car_lock = self._car_locks.setdefault(car.car_id, threading.RLock())
195	
196	        with car_lock:
197	            yield
198

23	def build_system(strategy=None) -> ElevatorControlSystem:
24	    # Two cars in a 0..10 building. car-A parked at 0, car-B parked at 10.
25	    cars = [
26	        ElevatorCar(car_id="car-A", min_floor=0, max_floor=10, current_floor=0),
27	        ElevatorCar(car_id="car-B", min_floor=0, max_floor=10, current_floor=10),

29	    return ElevatorControlSystem(cars, min_floor=0, max_floor=10, strategy=strategy)

32	def run_to_idle(system: ElevatorControlSystem, max_ticks: int = 100):
33	    """Tick until every car is parked, collecting the stop events in order."""
34	    events = []
35	
36	    for _ in range(max_ticks):
37	        batch = system.tick()
38	        events.extend(batch)
39	        car_states = [system._require_car(cid).state for cid in ("car-A", "car-B")]
40	
41	        if all(s is CarState.IDLE for s in car_states):
42	            break
43	
44	    return events

47	def case_1_dispatch_and_scan() -> None:
48	    system = build_system()
49	
50	    # Passenger on floor 3 wants UP. Nearest car is car-A (at 0). Then a rider in
51	    # car-A picks floor 8, and a rider picks floor 5 too.
52	    assigned = system.request_elevator(3, Direction.UP)
53	    assert assigned == "car-A", f"expected car-A, got {assigned}"
54	    system.select_floor("car-A", 8)
55	    system.select_floor("car-A", 5)
56	
57	    events = run_to_idle(system)
58	    floors_in_order = [e.floor for e in events if e.car_id == "car-A"]
59	    # SCAN going up serves 3, then 5, then 8 in ascending order.
60	    assert floors_in_order == [3, 5, 8], f"SCAN order wrong: {floors_in_order}"
61	    print(f"Case 1 dispatch + SCAN order {floors_in_order} -> OK")

Let me dig deeper into WHY. The claimed race is in `_orient_from_idle` choosing a wrong direction. Let me check whether that's even reachable.

The key: `_orient_from_idle` picks `min(self._stops, key=...)` — nearest stop. All stops are above floor 0, so direction is always UP regardless of which thread orients first or what subset of `_stops` is visible. The "wrong direction" race the document describes cannot actually occur here because all floors are on the same side. content.text:
Very revealing. Two key findings:

1. The race the document describes (wrong direction) is essentially unreachable in this scenario — all 47 floors are above floor 0, so `_orient_from_idle` always picks UP regardless of interleaving. The test's assertion `car_obj.direction is Direction.UP` would hold whether or not the lock exists.

2. There's a REAL concurrency bug (`RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration` in `_orient_from_idle`), but it only appears with floors on both sides and aggressive switch interval — and even then the queue never lost stops, because `set.add` is GIL-atomic. Critically, in the actual test (`select_floor`, all floors > 0, default switch interval) this never fires.

Let me now confirm the most important point: does the actual committed test pass with the no-op lock base class? I already showed it does (0 failures). Let me make this airtight by literally running the test file's exact test but swapping the system class, and also confirm `set.add`/`discard` atomicity means the set-uniqueness invariant is GIL-protected regardless of lock.

# Reproduce the EXACT committed test body but with the lock-free base class.
import threading
from elevator_service import ElevatorControlSystem
from models import ElevatorCar, CarState, Direction

def drain_single(system, car_id, max_ticks=200):
    events=[]
    for _ in range(max_ticks):
        events.extend(system.tick())
        if system._require_car(car_id).state is CarState.IDLE: break
    return events

passes=0
for _ in range(50):
    car = ElevatorCar(car_id='car-A', min_floor=0, max_floor=50, current_floor=0)
    system = ElevatorControlSystem([car], min_floor=0, max_floor=50)  # NO LOCK
    duplicate_floor=7; duplicate_pressers=40
    distinct_floors=[3,11,19,25,33,41,48]
    total=duplicate_pressers+len(distinct_floors)
    barrier=threading.Barrier(total); errors=[]; lk=threading.Lock()
    def press(floor):
        barrier.wait()
        try: system.select_floor('car-A', floor)
        except BaseException as e:
            with lk: errors.append(e)
    threads=[threading.Thread(target=press,args=(duplicate_floor,)) for _ in range(duplicate_pressers)]
    threads+=[threading.Thread(target=press,args=(f,)) for f in distinct_floors]
    for t in threads: t.start()
    for t in threads: t.join()
    expected=frozenset(distinct_floors)|{duplicate_floor}
    pending=system.pending_stops('car-A')
    co=system._require_car('car-A')
    try:
        assert errors==[]
        assert pending==expected
        assert co.state is CarState.MOVING
        assert co.direction is Direction.UP
        assert any(f>co.current_floor for f in pending)
        served=[e.floor for e in drain_single(system,'car-A')]
        assert served==sorted(expected)
        passes+=1
    except AssertionError:
        pass
print('Committed-test assertions PASS with NO lock:', passes, '/ 50')
"

I have enough evidence to render a verdict. Let me summarize my findings against each rubric item.

**Summary of evaluation:**

1. **Concurrency correctness**: The per-car lock IS implemented (Template Method `_lock` hook overridden in `ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem`). No deadlock, no lock leak, double-lock idiom is correct, slow work (StopEvent collection) kept outside lock. The lock is correct *as written*. BUT — the claimed race ("wrong direction from half-updated set") is largely a phantom: `set.add`/`in`/`discard` are GIL-atomic so the uniqueness/no-lost-stop invariant holds with OR without the lock. There IS a real latent bug — `_orient_from_idle` iterates `self._stops` via `min(...)` which can raise `RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration` under true concurrency — but the lock does prevent it (the lock is what makes that compound op safe). So the lock has real value, just not the value the doc claims.

2. **Concurrency test strength**: WEAK. I empirically proved the test passes 50/50 (and 200/200) against the lock-free base class. It cannot distinguish thread-safe from broken. The `direction is UP` assertion is satisfied by any interleaving because all floors are above floor 0.

3-8: §3.5 present and complete; §4.1 blocks complete; §6 matches disk (no drift, the only `...` is the labeled skeleton); patterns earned; mermaid exactly 2 blocks, no semicolons, both parse; Python idioms clean, no bare except, one domain base.

Let me write the final findings.

findings.problem: The concurrency test does NOT prove the per-car lock is load-bearing. I ran the EXACT test body against the lock-free base class (ElevatorControlSystem with the no-op _lock) and it passed 50/50 and 200/200 trials. Reason: (a) the only mutated invariant — set membership — is GIL-atomic (set.add/in/discard never lose or duplicate an element regardless of locking), so 'queue == expected_stops' holds with or without the lock; and (b) every pressed floor (3,7,11,19,25,33,41,48) is ABOVE the car's start floor (0), so _orient_from_idle always picks Direction.UP for ANY thread interleaving — the assertion `car_obj.direction is Direction.UP` is trivially satisfied even with zero locking. A test that passes identically against the broken (lock-free) and fixed (thread-safe) implementations proves nothing about the lock. The doc repeatedly overclaims it 'proved' the per-car lock made the add-and-orient atomic.
findings.fix: Make the test actually fail without the lock. (1) Add a parametrized/duplicate variant that runs the SAME barrier scenario against the lock-free ElevatorControlSystem and asserts it is NOT reliably safe (or assert the thread-safe one passes where a lock-free one demonstrably corrupts). (2) Use floors on BOTH sides of current_floor (e.g. start at floor 25, press a mix above and below) so the committed direction genuinely depends on atomicity of read-add-orient — then the lock's effect on the direction assertion is observable. (3) To expose the real race (RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration in _orient_from_idle, see other finding), the lock-free run will throw; assert the thread-safe run is clean.

findings.problem: The race the entire concurrency narrative is built on (two threads 'orient from a half-updated set' and the car ends up 'pointed at nothing' / a 'lost state write') does not actually occur in this design as a corruption: set.add and the direction/state assignments are GIL-atomic, and the worst case is a benign last-writer-wins on direction that still points at a real pending stop. The genuine, reproducible concurrency bug is different and unmentioned: _orient_from_idle does `min(self._stops, key=...)`, iterating the set while OTHER threads call self._stops.add(floor) — under real contention this raises `RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration`. I reproduced this deterministically with floors on both sides and a low sys.setswitchinterval against the lock-free base class. The per-car lock does fix it, but the write-up names the wrong mechanism, so a reader cannot tell the lock is actually necessary.
findings.fix: Correct the §9 / §5.5.D narrative to name the true invariant the lock protects: iterating _stops in _orient_from_idle (and _stops_in_current_direction / _open_doors_here's discard) concurrently with adds, which raises 'set changed size during iteration' without a lock. Then point the strengthened test at exactly that (concurrent press during an orient on a multi-sided stop set). Optionally snapshot the set inside _orient_from_idle (min over a list copy) so the single-threaded core is also robust.

findings.problem: The `direction` parameter is part of the select() signature but is never used by either NearestCarStrategy or LeastBusyStrategy. It is justifiable as a forward-looking seam (directional/zone strategies), and the doc's §9 twists rely on it, but as shipped it is an unused parameter on every implementation.
findings.fix: Either keep it and add a one-line comment that it is reserved for direction-aware strategies (the doc already implies this), or drop it from the v1 interface to avoid an unused-arg smell. Low priority — leave if the extensibility story is the intent.

findings.problem: The §9 code block shows the press() helper catching `except BaseException as exc: errors.append(exc)` with NO lock around the shared `errors` list, while the actual committed test correctly guards appends with a threading.Lock (test_elevator.py:233, 240).
